recommendations.riskRationale: All six entities carry AdministratorAccess (Action: *, Resource: *), granting unrestricted access to every AWS service including IAM, KMS, STS, and Organizations. Consolidation is technically trivial but the proliferation itself is the security concern — each entity represents an independent blast-radius vector for full account compromise.
recommendations.rationale: All entities are policy-identical, each bearing only AdministratorAccess. Rather than a straightforward merge, this cluster demands a purpose audit: most of these roles appear to duplicate administrative access without differentiated trust policies, session constraints, or permission boundaries. Before consolidating, each entity's actual usage, trust relationship, and originating use case must be validated. Roles that are unused or redundant should be deleted outright, not merged.

recommendations.riskRationale: CDK CloudFormation execution roles are scoped by trust policy to CloudFormation service principals and are managed by CDK bootstrap. Merging this into a human or SSO role would break CDK deploy pipelines and conflate service-automation trust with human identity, violating separation of duties.
recommendations.rationale: This role is a CDK bootstrap artifact with a CloudFormation service trust relationship. It must remain isolated from human-assumable roles regardless of policy overlap. Recommend replacing AdministratorAccess with a scoped CDK execution policy to reduce blast radius of infrastructure deployments.

recommendations.riskRationale: AWSReservedSSO_ roles are managed by AWS IAM Identity Center and cannot be manually assumed or modified. Their trust policy is bound to the SSO SAML provider. Any attempt to merge other roles into this entity or redirect users to it from outside SSO is unsupported and would fail.
recommendations.rationale: AWS IAM Identity Center reserved roles are system-managed and exist only to surface permission sets assigned in the SSO console. They cannot be the target of manual role assumption or consolidation. Human admins should access the account exclusively through SSO. The existence of non-SSO admin roles (wheel, RescorCloudAdministrator, RescorOrganizationAdministrator, MasterSetup_Role) alongside SSO admin access suggests redundant privilege paths that bypass SSO governance.

recommendations.riskRationale: All three roles are policy-identical with AdministratorAccess. Consolidation adds no new permissions, but retaining AdministratorAccess on any human-assumable role is inherently HIGH risk. This consolidation reduces the number of full-admin blast-radius vectors from three to one.
recommendations.rationale: RescorCloudAdministrator, RescorOrganizationAdministrator, and MasterSetup_Role are semantically distinct by name but policy-identical. If all three serve human operators in the same account with no trust-policy differentiation, they are pure redundancy. Consolidate to a single break-glass or operational admin role, enforce MFA conditions and session duration limits in the trust policy, and route day-to-day access through the SSO permission set instead. MasterSetup_Role should be reviewed for whether it was a one-time bootstrap role that can now be deleted entirely.

recommendations.riskRationale: IAM Groups with AdministratorAccess grant full-account privileges to every group member. Group membership is often less audited than role assumption, making this a hidden privilege escalation path.
recommendations.rationale: The 'wheel' group is a Linux/Unix convention repurposed as an IAM group granting AdministratorAccess to its members. IAM users in this group gain admin rights without assuming a role, bypassing CloudTrail AssumeRole events and making activity harder to attribute. Best practice is to remove AdministratorAccess from the group, migrate members to role assumption (preferably via SSO), and deprecate the group. It should not be merged with roles but eliminated as an access pattern.

analysis.summary: All six entities are policy-identical with AdministratorAccess, making technical consolidation trivial, but the real risk is the proliferation of six independent full-admin blast-radius vectors across different access patterns (SSO, CDK automation, direct group membership, and manual role assumption) — the priority action is to delete unused roles, enforce SSO as the sole human admin path, scope the CDK execution role, and reduce the three custom admin roles (RescorCloudAdministrator, RescorOrganizationAdministrator, MasterSetup_Role) to a single audited break-glass role with MFA and session constraints.

recommendations.riskRationale: AGTest-AppRole1 and AGTest-AppRole2 have identical managed policy sets (AGTest-CloudWatchLogs, AGTest-EC2Describe, AGTest-S3ReadOnly) and no inline policies. Merging introduces zero permission changes — the consolidated role is an exact duplicate of either source role.
recommendations.rationale: These two roles are functionally identical with 100% policy overlap and no inline policies differentiating them. Maintaining both creates unnecessary administrative overhead, doubles the audit surface, and provides no security boundary benefit. They should be collapsed into a single role; all principals currently assuming AGTest-AppRole1 should be redirected to AGTest-AppRole2 (or a renamed canonical role).

recommendations.riskRationale: The only permission AGTest-DataRole1 would gain upon merging into AGTest-DataRole2 is AGTest-EC2Describe, which is a read-only EC2 describe policy. No write, IAM, KMS, or other sensitive permissions are introduced.
recommendations.rationale: AGTest-DataRole1 and AGTest-DataRole2 share 3 of 4 policies (75% overlap: AGTest-CloudWatchLogs, AGTest-RDSReadOnly, AGTest-S3ReadOnly). The only delta is that AGTest-DataRole2 additionally holds AGTest-EC2Describe. Consolidation is worth reviewing — if the principals assuming AGTest-DataRole1 have no operational need to describe EC2 resources, the separation is justified and should be preserved. If EC2 describe access is benign or expected for the data workload, merging eliminates a redundant role at negligible risk.

recommendations.riskRationale: The additional permission is RDS read-only; however, granting application roles access to database read endpoints without a confirmed need violates least-privilege even if the technical risk classification is low.
recommendations.rationale: Although the App roles (EC2Describe) and Data roles (RDSReadOnly) both share AGTest-CloudWatchLogs and AGTest-S3ReadOnly, they represent distinct functional boundaries — application-tier compute access vs. data-tier database access. Merging them would grant app principals RDS read access and data principals EC2 describe access without clear justification. These role families should remain separate to preserve the application/data trust boundary, even though all permissions involved are read-only.

analysis.summary: The four roles reduce to two canonical role families — App (AppRole1/AppRole2 are identical and should be merged immediately) and Data (DataRole1/DataRole2 differ only by EC2Describe and warrant a brief review before consolidation) — yielding a potential reduction from 4 roles to 2 with no write or sensitive-service permissions introduced.
